communityVoices.quote: cornell is known for perfection. i'll grant it that. in it's brevity, it misses the nuance of more messed up, space/angst-ridden shows in the '80s
This show has made a 4,200 mile trip from Atlanta, GA to Sturgis, SD for the 2004 Rally, with this show playing most of the way.

whyThisShow: Cornell 5/8/77 is one of the most-circulated tapes in Dead history and has been officially released as part of May 1977: Get Shown the Light. The second set — Scarlet Begonias into Fire on the Mountain, Estimated Prophet, St. Stephen, Not Fade Away, and Morning Dew — is what the community keeps coming back to. One listener described the Dancin' in the Streets as "almost not human" [source: 'The Dancin In The Streets is almost not human, and the Scarlet-Fire is killer.']. The Scarlet > Fire transition draws the most attention, with multiple reviewers calling it out specifically. One dissenting voice argues the show's reputation rests on a kind of polished professionalism and points elsewhere for rawness: "don't get me wrong, i'm a fan. but check out binghampton '77" [source: "don't get me wrong, i'm a fan. but check out binghampton '77"], but that view sits against the broad weight of community reverence documented across nearly 300 Archive reviews.
listenFor: The Scarlet Begonias > Fire on the Mountain is where most listeners focus, and it has generated the sharpest opinions — one reviewer singles out Keith Godchaux's playing during the transition as a distraction: "His incessant 'premature fire overlay' is such a fucking drag when Jerry, Bob and Phil are galloping through the cosmos" [source: 'His incessant "premature fire overlay" is such a fucking drag when Jerry, Bob and Phil are galloping through the cosmos where the only foot hold available is planet to planet!']. The Dancin' in the Streets earlier in the set also draws notice [source: 'The Dancin In The Streets is almost not human']. Don't sleep on the first set: one listener credits the Loser specifically as the song that got them into the Dead, calling it "the best version of any song I've heard them do" [source: 'I think it is the best version of any song Ive heard them do']. Morning Dew closes the set, though one experienced listener notes it is not necessarily the best version and points to other years for comparison [source: 'is it the best morning dew ever? hardly. there are other greats, commendable for attributes that did not exist during the spring of '77'].
context: This show was recorded from the soundboard at Barton Hall, Cornell University on May 8, 1977, during what collectors call the 77-peak era. The recording circulated for decades as one of the most-traded tapes in the lot before the band's organization officially released it as May 1977: Get Shown the Light / Cornell 5/8/77, confirming its canonical place in the catalog. One reviewer notes this was part of a tour of short, concentrated shows: "the second set is little over an hour! but that short set meant business" [source: 'the second set is little over an hour! but that short set meant business']. The SBD source (identifier: gd77-05-08.sbd.hicks.4982.sbeok.shnf) is the basis for most of what people have heard.
funFact: One listener's entire introduction to the Dead came from a scratched-up copy of just Disc 1, kept loose in a college roommate's Jeep for years, with no idea the second set existed: "Man, I wonder if the rest of the show is this good?" [source: 'I remember him saying Man, I wonder if the rest of the show is this good?']
headline: Barton Hall, May '77, on a soundboard that held up.
